figures.caption: Figure 1: Problem setting (see §2 for more details). We consider a minimal solvable setting with two binary concepts (shape, color) giving us four classes C := { , , , }. (A) Schematic of perfectly abstract representation where shape concept vectors ured and ublue have cosine similarity αshape = 1. (B) In the minimal model, introducing an orthogonal interaction mode (SC, green) with eigenvalue λSC lowers the abstraction αshape, hence we refer to it as the “noise” direction. (C) The abstraction trajectory of a deep ConvNet trained on the 3dshapes dataset [21] exhibits nontrivial dynamics, which is not captured by prior theories. In particular, its trajectory is non-monotonic and it plateaus well below α = 1 (see §D.2.2 for experiment details). In this paper, our aim is a dynamical theory of abstraction which captures such behavior. (D) Abstraction and probe generalization of deep ReLU network trained on synthetic data (see §D.1.1 for experimental details). P red→blue

Figure 2: Analytic laws governing abstraction dynamics in the minimal model. All solid colored lines correspond to numerical simulations. (A) shows trajectory of α(t) in an L = 1 hidden layer network for varying weight initialization. For all initializations, α(t) converges to the red terminal value Theorem 4. Meanwhile, the maximum abstraction reached over the trajectory decreases with initialization scale (Theorem 5). For very small initialization, α(t) reaches and stays near 1 for a long time. (B) Abstraction interpolates layerwise in L = 6 layer network, where ℓ is the layer index; lighter colors correspond to deeper layers. Abstraction improves in deeper layers. (C) Terminal abstraction in final layer increases with network depth L, where red limit is given by αΣy .

Figure 3: Empirical validation of the minimal model in convolutional networks. (A) We train small ResNets on a task constructed from the 3dshapes dataset [21] using binary shape and color latents, with n = 1000 examples per class while nuisance variables (floor color, wall color, size, orientation) vary randomly. For panels B-D, error bars show SEM across 20 seeds. (B) Maximum abstraction in last layer decreases with weight initialization scale σ2 w, in agreement with Theorem 5. (C) Final abstraction after training in last layer increases with network depth L, in agreement with Theorem 7. (D) Final abstraction after training increases layerwise in a depth L = 8 network, in agreement with Theorem 7 (i.e. deeper layers are more abstract than early layers).

Figure 5: Local GELU ablation improves concept abstraction and linear probe generalization in transformers. We apply Theorem 8 to transformers by using the local GELU ablation technique described in §5. (A) For visual concepts, we evaluate DINOv3 ViT-L/16 [39] on the 3dshapes task described in §3.3. Local GELU ablation improves or preserves shape abstraction in every layer compared to baseline. Also, baseline abstraction improves layerwise which agrees with Theorem 7. (B) For language concepts, we analyze the final residual stream of Gemma 4 E2B [40] evaluated on 18 bilingual concepts (e.g. English-Spanish), each instantiated by 80 word pairs (e.g. Man, Hombre). Local GELU ablation improves abstraction for all 18 bilingual concepts (i.e. all points lie above diagonal). (C) The same intervention improves or preserves linear probe generalization for 17/18 concepts, with an average accuracy improvement of ∼1 percentage point.

Figure 6: Abstraction increases along macaque ventral visual stream. We test the prediction of Theorem 7 that deeper cortical layers should exhibit greater abstraction using a subset of recordings in macaque V4 and IT from the public Majaj-Hong dataset [41]. (A) We construct a 2 × 2 factor design around the concepts of naturalness and limbedness. (B) In a pooled site-matched analysis, both concepts are significantly more abstract in IT than in V4 (error bars are 95% bootstrap intervals). (C) Linear probes trained to decode both concepts generalize significantly better in IT than in V4.

Figure 7: Variable-projected readout versus ordinary equal-timescale readout training. We compare full-batch gradient descent on the full loss L(Wr,Z) against gradient descent on the reduced loss L∗(Z) = minWr L(Wr,Z), corresponding to Assump. 1. Curves show mean ± SEM over 12 random seeds. A. Shape abstraction α(t). B. Training loss. C. Test accuracy. D. Loss gap for the full-gradient-descent baseline. The variable-projected method has zero loss gap by construction.
Table 1: The 80 ordered English–Spanish word pairs used for the English–Spanish concept in the Gemma ablation experiments.
